speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape number 4. Today is March 29th 1996, interview with Fred Roer. Would you continue in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] after we undressed there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] first of all led into an area like a-- like a conveyor belt. It was barracks after barracks, just divided with hardly any separation. You went in there. The first thing you went through, you went up the little ramp. There was a guy standing with-- with barber shears and barber scissors. And he would cut all the hair on your body, from top to bottom. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that you would go to the next area, where somebody of the guards were, and would look into every cavity of your body, whether you were hiding something or not. From there you would go into a shower area, where the people would be packed in. Then all of a sudden the showerheads would come on with ice-cold water. And to more-- to give it more power or whatever you want to call it, there would be guys standing with firehoses all around and would train them hoses on you. From there, you would go into the next area. There would be somebody standing with a-- a sponge and have some kind of a lye salt solution or what it was to rub you down for delousing. The next area you would go in somebody would throw a-- a uniform at you, a pair of pants and-- and a top and a-- a rag, like an underwear. The uniform could be for a size somebody double your height or half your size. From there, you would go on and they would tell you, when you get out you can exchange the right that it fits you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next area would be they would give you a pair of shoes, so-called shoes, which was a wooden sole with a canvas top over it. And you would get two rags about 12 by 12 as-- you would wrap them around your feet as-- as stockings. Then the next area you would go, you would get two strips with a-- with a number on it, which would be your prison number. And then the next-- the next area, there would somebody stand and grab your arm and put your-- tattoo your number on you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you display your tattoo for the camera?

speaker: interviewee
text: This.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you can continue. Describe how you received that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They just grab your arm and somebody just starts writing your number on there and it hurts a lot, just-- just one after the other. And after that, you would be out there and then you would exchange your uniform. They would give you a needle and some thread, and you would put your number also on your jacket and your pants. And now this whole procedure like I described it to you took two nights and three days. You would be standing there naked more or less all the time, in the fall and up in Auschwitz, where it gets quite cold too. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but no food. Nobody would care. They just would count you every few minutes that everybody is there. And after that, you would-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after that, we were put into quarantine, so-called quarantine. The quarantine was run by the kapos. The kapos were-- in the quarantine were all criminals. And they would do whatever the Nazis told them, the SS. You never saw one without a whip. They would whip you for no reason at all. The barracks were three tiers, just wooden planks you would lay on. You had about a foot, a foot and a half to lay. And during the day, you would be put out in the exercise yard. And all you would do is run around, hop like a frog, or-- or roll around, just to keep you busy. In one corner of the exercise yard was a pile of rocks, of heavy rocks. They would tell you to carry it to the next corner. So for-- for about 8 to 10 days in this quarantine, all you do is just being harassed. And the food in there was-- the-- these kapos would steal half of it. You would get half of the ration, which was already small to start with. And this was a ongoing thing for about like I said 8 to 10 days. After that we were loaded, all of a sudden loaded--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you leave that, can you describe a particular instance where you saw the brutality of Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the brutality-- one of the cruel-- cruelest scene, one of them that I saw was when we arrived in Auschwitz. The other transport, like I said, there were men, women, and children coming from-- from Europe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, when they separated the men and the women and the children, there was one man. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He went over and wanted to kiss his wife goodbye and hold his baby for-- a child of about a year, year and a half, would hold her for a little while. And one of the Nazis says you don't have-- give that child back to your wife. When he gave it back, he went over and wanted to kiss-- kiss this baby. And the Nazi just grabbed it away from his wife and stick it on a bayonet and gave it over to the guy. Here is your baby. Take her along. And the woman actually was screaming and crying and putting on-- getting on or whatever you would call it. And all the Nazi did shot her right there, then and there, and killed her. And this husband [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lost it completely and jumped at that Nazi. And at the same time, three other guys came with the bayonet and killed him right there. So this was the first impression we got from Auschwitz, or I got.

speaker: interviewer
text: So today, like I-- today I question myself whether these things happened, because this is such a brutality, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which you can't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which is a-- which a normal human being can hardly believe. So this is what I said I never talk about it, because somebody will say, he makes that up. That's something what no human being would do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is one of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Other things what happened, which was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two people tried to escape and were caught and brought back into the cam. To-- to make an example, they put them on-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tied them up on a-- like a cross and had their arms spread and the legs spread and let them hang there for-- for three or four days until-- until they died. And coming by there, every kapo would-- would use his bullwhip and would hit these people hanging there to die, just hanging out like laundry. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Things like that which occurred there, later on you wouldn't even react to it anymore, because you yourself became an animal. You weren't human being anymore. Your whole mind was set on getting extra food or-- or to survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Other people couldn't take it. They would go out into the fence, which was the electric wire, which was loaded with about 5,000 volt of-- of electricity. They just would electrocute themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were things going on at every time, any time. Some guard didn't like you, he would tell you to go over there to-- to pick up a rock and then he would shoot you in the back and say you were trying to escape. So if he didn't like you, he would shoot you or kill you or would take the bullwhip and hit you 'til you were dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- what-- every time, what all they did was always counting everybody every morning, every night, every time you went to work. Every time where there was a group moving, you would be counted. You had to-- you had to stand there in-- in columns of five people all the time. Every-- the first thing in the morning was if somebody died at night, you had to lay him there so that the five was there to count. And if the count didn't come out, then you stood there for hours. Maybe somebody couldn't count right or somebody wasn't there or something, somebody didn't see somebody dead in bed or something, so the count didn't come out, you would wait 'til that came out all right. In the wintertime when it was bitter, bitter cold, you would all stand out there. People would just fall over. And nobody was-- could even go close and pick them up or anything. You had to stand on-- at attention all the time. And everything what they did there was a point of harassment in any way, in which way. After 10 days or 8 days-- I don't remember exact dates-- we were loaded with about 500 on-- onto army trucks and driven away from the quarantine and no idea where we were going. And then we arrived in an area what was fenced in with electric fence. And there was a brick building in there and-- and a barrack. And then we were put into that building. We're told next morning we have to go to work. We were fitted out with a-- a miner's lamp and got a second uniform. And the next morning, then we were divided into three groups for the first shift, second shift, or night shift. I ended up in the night shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- the next day we were-- first of all, we had to clean the camp after we were assigned where to sleep. There were bunk beds there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the whole thing was run like-- like a military. We had to clean our beds. The beds had to-- when he stood on one side, each bed had to be at the same height and all the same junk just to harass you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we had to clean the-- the camp itself. We were told we had to enlarge the camp. We had to build barracks and make terraces so that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] different levels of the camp, because it was on a hill. And eventually the camp was enlarged to 1,200 from 500 when we first got there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: This camp was called Janina. I don't know what they called it in Polish. And that was a coal mine. The coal mine was called Janina too. And that town-- there was a little town, which was called Janina, where the Polish miners lived. The next day we were marched to the mine-- to the mine up there and got into the elevator and went down. I don't know how many feet, actually, but quite a way down. And once we got down, there was a very wet, wet coal mine. And we were divided into different groups with different miners. The section, each section-- there were six sections in that area. And each section was overseen by a-- by a German engineer, a mine engineer. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] naturally, the miners all were Polish, Polish workers because it was right in that town. There again we were-- we had to load the wagons either with coal or with the-- with the rocks they cut out. Sometimes there was two feet-- you had to lay down in the water and shuffle under the conveyor belts, which would then go into the little coal wagons. And the coal wagons would be sent by little locomotives outside. There were big piles of-- of stone or coal. And then from there, the coal would be sent into Germany most probably or the-- or even to-- around there there were different camps, which were working on war industry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in the coal mine where I was working. Like I said, we had to lay down and shovel the coal sometimes in water and it was very wet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There again, the food was a little better than before, but still not enough to survive. You would get the tar coffee in the morning. You would get the water soup, which was a little bit thicker, sometimes was a vegetable and a potato in there. You would get this two or three slices of bread every day and the soup at night and before we went to work. And then-- and then noontime, when we were in the camp, we would sleep. We would come back to camp about 7 o'clock or 8 o'clock. They would let us sleep 'til about noon, and that was the time. Then we would be-- have to work in the camp building, enlarging it, cleaning it, all different things [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We would be marched to the-- to the coal mine with the SS guards around us and they would use their back of the guns to pick you or push you. And there again, we wouldn't get enough food. Luckily, the-- these miners were living in the country. They were raising chickens. They were-- had-- had little garden plots or something. They didn't have much, but they had extra food. They didn't have any clothing, for instance. To get extra food, I would sell my second uniform. I would come out of the-- of the mine and I would tell-- I would go down with two uniforms on and would sell him one. He would dye the uniform, use it as underwear, use it as outerwear too. The women would alterate it a little bit. And for that, we would then get food from the miner again. To substitute extra food, this is one way I was doing it. We did same with the shoes. And then we would say we lost it or somebody stole it. You knew you would get a whipping from the guards, but there was the choice, either food or-- or dying. The same we would-- these miners there were-- at home didn't have any electricity. They would use the carbide which was in the lamps to light their house, so we would sell the carbide to them. And-- in front of the elevator down in the shaft down where we were working, there was a big pile of used-up carbide. We would go through the pile and pick out all the little pieces and put them in our lamp and would sell the big pieces for food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sometimes--

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we would even sell the lamp and exchange the lamp. His was broken and we would bring it to camp and say, well, the lamp isn't working right. Each lamp had a-- a little chain on it with a number, so you would switch the numbers over. And this way, any which way you could, you would sell and try extra food. Also, what we would do is I would call it a-- a kind of sabotage. But nobody was organizing it, just a feeling for yourselves to get-- to get-- feel better. We would mix the rocks with the coal. We would-- there-- there was some places where you needed winches to lead the-- the coal wagons down steep hills from one level to the other. We-- the winches had all steel cable, which were woven-- woven little-- little wires, little steel wires woven together into a cable. And sometimes we would start breaking them wires. They would wear out. And then when-- we would break them on purpose. And then when the German engineer came, this overseer, we would say, look here. This wire is breaking pretty soon. You better get it replaced. And they didn't have that much wire to replace it that often. Oh, he would say, that still holds.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what we would do, we would-- instead of two wagons, we would put three wagons on that wire then have it run all full force down the hill. Then we'd pull the brakes. That thing would snap and these wagons would go down into the main road, where they would be switched usually and would tear the whole place apart down there with the force it hit the wall and everything. Then it was two, three days that they couldn't use this area of the-- of the mine. So this was little things. I won't say it was organized. But it was things we did to-- to satisfy ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were electric wires running around and we always were warned, don't touch them in any way, you will be electrocuted. So we would take the shovel and throw the shovel against it without holding it, you know. And it would shorten out the area. So they never knew who did it, because you always did it either with somebody you knew real well or you didn't do it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you getting any information about what was going on around Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would get information from the Polish worker. Some of them would listen to radio, England or whatever. We would get German information, naturally. The Germans would tell us we're winning the war and all that thing. But we-- we would have some ideas what was going on, not-- not really, but just ideas, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the-- the coal mines?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the coal mine I was from fall of '43 'til January of '45, when the Russians were coming into this area and Auschwitz was liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Auschwitz, we were-- first we went on a forced march for about 8 to 10 days. These forced marches, I don't know whether I have to explain it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went from where to where? Explain your experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went from Auschwitz to Gleiwitz. Gleiwitz is a city in Germany. The marches naturally were just-- just death marches. Either you-- you could march or you couldn't march. If you fell behind, there was a so-called medic in the back with his gun. And he would just push you into the-- into the ditch on the side of the road, would shoot you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you at that point? What was your strength like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- my strength wasn't too bad at that time, because like I said before, I would take chances to get extra food. For instance, one time we-- when we went back to the camp before they entered through the camp, sometimes they would search us, the guards. And I was caught with a bag of sugar one time and I got a beating for it and I got 10-- 10 whips for it. But I still kept on doing it, because that was the only way to survive. And that was one thing what happened. What happened also in Janina is the only person I know who really escaped, did escaped. And the one who-- who escaped, I found out later on after he was gone he was a Polish army-- a Polish quite high officer. And he got in contact with the Polish Underground. And one of the-- which one I don't know-- one of these coal miners got involved in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put him in one of the wagons what went outside and dumped out and covered it with coal. And when he was dumped out there, he rolled down on that hill. And on the bottom of the hill was the-- the partisans were waiting for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was the only one who I know who was able to escape. And for that, we had to stand at attention when we weren't working for three full days in-- in the middle of winter and no food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the the guards were sent back to Auschwitz. We got new guards, which were a lot worse than the ones we had. Then when the ones we had before came back, they were even worse than the ones we got in replacement, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it happened on their shift that the guy-- when the guy escaped. That's the only escape I know of which was successful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then from Auschwitz-- we coming back to the march there-- when we were marching during the day we would be marching. And at night we would be put into a barn. And if the farmer had some of his pig food he was boiling for his pig's left, we would be fed. If he didn't have any, we wouldn't get anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then in the morning, we would march again. And when we-- we were marching-- for instance, you can think of the German mentality at that time when the war was practically lost. The Russians were right there. The-- the Germans-- the Germans-- in one little town, a woman came out with a-- like a drawer full of glowing ash from the oven, from the stove, and threw that over us and called us dirty Jews. So that was still the mentality of Hitler's time. And that was in January of '45, and already everything was more or less lost. Then we went to Gleiwitz. And in Gleiwitz, again we-- we were put on open coal wagons, about 100, 150 people into a coal wagon. We got about three slices of bread, and then off we went again. We didn't know where we were going again there. There again, we didn't know where we were headed to. And there were-- like I said, the coal wagons were open and it started snowing. And everybody was just cold, freezing in there. And to-- to get any water, we would pick the snow from each other's back and use this as-- as liquid so we would have something to drink or liquid in our-- in our system. And--
